summary:
Following the [[event:2699|Chernobyl nuclear disaster]] of April 1986, Soviet authorities established a 30-kilometer exclusion zone around the destroyed reactor, evacuating approximately 350,000 people from the surrounding area, including the city of Pripyat (population 49,000) and the town of Chernobyl itself. The evacuation was initially characterized by official minimization of the danger: residents were told they would be able to return in a few days, leading many to leave without essential belongings.

The exclusion zone, established under emergency conditions, became a permanent feature of the post-disaster landscape. Soviet cleanup workers — collectively called liquidators — worked in hazardous conditions to contain radioactive contamination and build the concrete sarcophagus covering the damaged reactor, which was completed in November 1986. Estimates of the number of liquidators range from 600,000 to 800,000, many of whom received significant radiation doses. The long-term health effects of the disaster, particularly the thyroid cancer epidemic in children exposed to radioactive iodine, became the subject of extensive scientific study. The abandoned city of Pripyat and the broader exclusion zone became, paradoxically, a de facto wildlife sanctuary as nature reclaimed the empty landscape, and eventually a site of dark tourism.
